content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-4441 322-6444

August 21, 1981
4-81-032

Linda Best
146 Via Copla
Alamo, CA 94507

Dear Linda:

This letter is sent in response to your letter of July 17,
1981, in which you requested clarification and ampliation
of my advice letter to you dated June 26, 1981. Generally,
our policy is not to advise regarding future hypothetical
situations since the analysis of conflicts of interest depends
heavily on the concrete factual situation presented. However,
I will attempt to answer your questions. If in the future
you need advice as to a particular decision, we will be most
happy to provide you with it.

As you noted, my understanding of the situation as
reflected in my letter was that your duties included assisting
in the presentation of and presenting COLAB's position to
governmental officials and decision-making bodies in an
attempt to influence governmental decisions. However, if
your duties are purely administrative, my analysis of the
application of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(B) to
your situation should be disregarded. On the other hand, if
your position with COLAB does require your participation in
the research, development, implementation or preparation of
policy or position statements or the promotion or discussion
of COLAB's policies or positions with public officials (whether
or not you actually formulate the policies), my analysis
stands.

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(B) provides that
an official has a financial interest in a governmental decision
when "there is a nexus between the governmental decision and
the purpose for which the official received income." As you
noted, the word "nexus" means a tie, link, or connection;
accordingly, if a person is paid to promote or advocate the
policies or position of an individual or group, ne or she

Linda Best
August 21, 1981
Page Two


may not then participate in a governmental decision which
draws into consideration that policy or position. I have
enclosed a copy of a recent advice letter in which the "nexus"
test was applied.

     The underlying rationale for the "nexus" test is that
when a connection exists between a person's job and his or
her role as a public official, there is a presumption that
the value of the employee's services to the employer is
based at least in part on the fact that the employee is a
public official. If an employee is being paid to do something
similar to what she does as a public official, we presume
that the employer is getting something of value from the
situation. There is an inherent appearance of conflict as
well as an actual conflict when an official is being paid to
promote or represent certain positions, on the one hand, and
on the other, she is being called on to determine public
policies and positions in the same area.

     Before I apply this analysis to the situations you
raised in your letter, I would like to make the following
two points in response to concerns you expressed. First,
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3))B) does not apply only
to the situation where an official has been hired to promote
a specific proposal or the situation where an official sits
on a governmental body with the sole purpose of representing
his or her employer's interest. It has a broader application,
as I discussed above. Second, to the extent that lines are
difficult to draw between those decisions in which you have
a conflict of interest and those in which you haven't, it
may reflect a certain overlapping between the concerns of
COLAB and the general role and function of the San Ramon
Valley Area Planning Commission (SRVAPC). The Political
Reform Act focuses on specific governmental decisions, and
any overall complexity, ambiguity, and incompatibility are
not addressed. Thus, the Act does not take into account the
broader meaning or implications of the existence of a conflict
with regard to particular decisions.

     The first set of situations you raised dealt with the
issue of slope density. As you noted correctly, if COLAB
took a position on a particular proposal before the SRVAPC,
your disqualification on the matter would be required. As
to the other possible factual circumstances, for the purposes
of Political Reform Act analysis, the distinguishing or
determining factor would be whether COLAB had a specific
policy or position which related to the content of the SRVAPC

Linda Best
August 21, 1981
Page Three


decision. In other words, it COLAB took the position that
slope density requirements should not be imposed, or that a
slope density ordinance should take a particular form or
approach, you should probably not participate in any SRVAPC
decision on slope density requirements. However, if a position
or policy on slope density was not taken by COLAB but could
perhaps be inferred from its other policies and positions,
disqualification would depend on whether it is clear or
apparent what COLAB's position would be.

As to your other examples, the conclusions would depend
on whether COLAB has either a specific policy on point with
the governmental decision or a general policy that clearly
implied a certain specific policy with reference to the
governmental decision. For example, you stated that COLAB
generally favors increased housing supply, and you asked
whether you could vote on a major housing development. If
the only policy of COLAB pertinent to the particular decision
was this general policy, whether disqualification was required
would depend on whether it could be clearly inferred from
the policy that this type of development should be approved.
The same analysis would apply to the other examples.

To summarize, the relevant factors are whether COLAB
has a specific policy or position that goes directly to the
content of a SRVAPC decision, or whether COLAB has a general
policy, the application of which would require a particular
SRVAPC decision even if there are countervailing factors.

I trust this letter provides you with further direction
and guidance. If I can be of further assistance with reference
to a particular, actual decision, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Diane Maura Fishrubn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosure

July 17, 1981

Ms. Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel, Legal Division
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Diane:

INTRODUCTION

The purpose of this letter is to respond to your letter dated
June 26, 1981. Specifically, I refer to your discussion
extending 2 Cal.Adm.Code Section 18702(b)(3)(B) to the facts
of my situation.

Part of the problem may relate to the fact that data I originally presented you had (at your request) only focused upon
the financial aspects of COLAB and its membership. Very
little attention was given to the specifics of my duties at
COLAB, the COLAB philosophy, COLAB positions and my volunteer
service as a San Ramon Valley Area Planning Comissioner.

I will attempt here to provide this added background and
would invite you to further review COLAB's publications and
the duties of a planning commission in Contra Costa County if
you think it is necessary in order to further clarify this
situation. My objective is to have you establish "bright
lines" between what is authorized and what is unauthorized
conduct.

SUPPLEMENTAL FACTS

My employment by COLAB is independent of my volunteer service
on the San Ramon Valley Area Planning Commission. The San
Ramon Valley is but a small part of the geographical area
of interest to COLAB. I wish very much to continue to serve
on and I believe that my service on the Commission will help
me at COLAB and that my working at COLAB will help me on the
SRVAPC.

COLAB's avowed purposes are:

A. To provide a forum for previously unrelated
interest groups to meet and exchange information;

Ms. Diane Maura Fishburn
July 17, 1981
Page 2

     B.   To educate its members and others how selected
national, state, regional and local actions can affect other
social and economic objectives (including housing, jobs and
economic growth);

     C.   To assure that these other social and economic con-
cerns are given equal weight in the decisional process along
with concerns such as environmental protection, overcrowding
and other planning objectives.

     In carrying out its objectives COLAB has produced several
studies and has taken formal positions on a wide variety of
topics in its nearly five year existence.  Examples of
COLAB's principal activities are outlined in the COLAB state-
ment provided with my letter of June 3, 1981. In addition,
COLAB has attempted to alert its membership to the issues
raised by a number of specific state, national, regional and
local proposals too numerous to mention.

     COLAB does not exist to "lobby" and, to the extent that I
understand your use of the term, personal contact with
officials is generally reserved to members of the Board of
Directors (not the Executive Secretary). Most of my duties
are administrative, monitoring and research. When the Board
of Directors or the Executive Committee act, I will transmit
their written position to others (including officials). I am
not authorized to negotiate or to act without direction from
the Board of Directors. My advice or opinions (if any) are
directed to the Board of Directors in much the same manner as
committee consultants relate to legislative committees.

                    PURPOSE OF MY EMPLOYMENT

     I clearly understand that Sections 87100 and 87103 provide
that an official must disqualify herself from a decision if
it would have "a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally", upon any source of
income. While I don't disagree that COLAB is a "source of
income" to me, I cannot understand how any decision I made on
the SRVAPC could either:

     (a)  Have a material financial effect upon COLAB (COLAB
          being a non-profit corporation); or

     (b)  Have a financial effect upon COLAB which was in any
          way distinguishable from its effect upon the public
          generally.

Ms. Diane Maura Fishburn
July 17, 1981
Page 3

To answer these points, you apparently rely upon
2 Cal.Adm.Code Section 18702(b)(3)(B) which provides that
"material financial effect" is defined to include those
situations where "there is a nexus between the governmental
decision and the purpose for which the official receives
income". I don't understand your application of this
regulation to my situation in light of the facts recited
above.

The term "nexus" is understood by me to mean a "tie or link."
While I can foresee circumstances where there may be a "link"
between a COLAB position and a particular vote on the SRVAPC
(as when COLAB has taken an official position on a particular
matter before the SRVAPC) I do not see how the requirement of
the regulation that there must also be a "link" between a
particular vote on the SRVAPC and the "purpose" for which I
am paid by COLAB. That is, there needs to be a nexus between the purpose for which the official received income and
the particular governmental decision. I cannot find such a
"nexus" in my situation.

Specifically, both COLAB and I affirm that I was not hired at
COLAB nor do I continue to work for COLAB for the purpose of
serving on the SRVAPC or voting on any proposal before the
SRVAPC whatsoever. I can see this regulation as being applicable only to a situation where an official has been specifically hired to promote a specific proposal or where he or she
was hired for the express purpose of sitting on a particular
Board or Commission to further an employer's interests.

Your analysis begins to suggest a new test that I should not
be allowed to vote on any matter which would simply further
the policies of COLAB.

AMBIGUITY

Even if we are to assume that the new facts I have presented
here do not change your opinion, I need clarification of your
intent. I have no trouble understanding statement that
I should not participate in any specific proposal before the
SRVAPC upon which COLAB has taken a specific position. Once
we go beyond this point the lines get rather hazy. The two
examples you provided may or may not help.

In the first example, you suggest that if COLAB took a position on the "issue of slope density requirements" then I
should refrain from voting on a particular slope density

Ms. Diane Maura Fishburn
July 17, 1981
Page 4

ordinance before the SRVAPC. Again, I understand your view
that if COLAB supported or opposed a particular proposed
ordinance then before the SRVAPC I should not be involved.
But what do I do in these hypothetical circumstances:

(1) COLAB suggests to all Bay Area governments (in one
widely distributed position paper) that all slope
density ordinances are inappropriate because they
tend to represent single purpose planning and are a
simplistic attempt to shortcut the case-by-case
planning process; or

(2) COLAB suggests (in a widely distributed position
paper) that in evaluating slope density ordinances
all Bay Area governments should be mindful of the
need to maximize the use of available land (where
urban infrastructure is reasonably available) in
order to relieve the housing crisis; or

(3) COLAB appears before the SRVAPC concerning a par-
ticular proposed slope density ordinance then
before the SRVAPC and suggest that, for example,
flexibility needs to be built into the ordinance to
recognize special topographical factors, housing
need or a particularly meritorious project; or

(4) COLAB is silent about slope density ordinances in
general but generally berates (over time) a wide
variety of open space proposals as single purpose
planning which tend to ignore other social and eco-
nomic issues including jobs, housing, economic
growth and property rights.

Your second example is not much more helpful than your first.
Specifically, you suggest that if COLAB "worked for changes
in the process by which governmental entities reviewed propo-
sals for developments", I would be precluded from par-
ticipating in planning commission decisions involving the
"process by which the commission reviewed development propo-
sals." Again, I clearly understand your opinion where COLAB
supported or opposed a particular procedural proposal which
was then pending before the SRVAPC. But what am I to do in
these hypothetical situations:

(A) COLAB generally repeatedly suggests that the
process by which governments review specific
development proposals is needlessly time con-

Ms. Diane Maura Fishburn
July 17, 1981
Page 5

suming and that these delays inflate the cost
of housing, and the SRVAPC is subsequently
asked to act upon either:

(i) A particular request to approve a project
without an environmental impact report or
a general plan change; or

(ii) A request, over staff objection, that a
matter be heard on a particular date
rather than continued 30 days; or

(iii) A series of proposals designed to expe-
dite commission deliberations and/or to
shift added duties to the zoning
administrator.

(B) COLAB spearheads a move to improve the governmental permit process generally in Contra
Costa County and the Board of Supervisors
appoints a task force to make recommendations
and the SRVAPC is asked to comment upon the
specifics of these recommended changes prior
to Board action.

As one can see, once the door is opened beyond the situation
where COLAB has taken a specific position on a specific proposal then pending before the SRVAPC, it is very hard to draw
the line.

I begin to wonder how often under your opinion that the
issues surrounding the "philosophy" or "general policies" of
COLAB may render me unable to serve because the matters
before the SRVAPC may raise specific examples of COLAB's
general concerns. Clear direction involves providing me
answers to your view concerning these not so hypothetical
situations:

(A) COLAB generally favors increased housing supply and
I am asked to vote for or against a major housing
development; or

(B) COLAB supports both infill development and urban
expansion to meet the Bay Area's housing needs
and the SRVAPC is asked to approve a major general
plan change allowing added housing adjacent to
existing development; or

Ms. Diane Maura Fishburn
July 17, 1981
Page 6

(C) COLAB generally questions whether proposals to pro-
tect agricultural land ignore the need for housing
and are actually designed to limit growth (and the
SRVAPC is asked to rezone a large tract of land for
exclusively agricultural purposes); or

(D) COLAB generally supports the creation of new jobs
and also supports increased housing supply upon the
basis that job growth will slow if housing is not
available and the SRVAPC is asked to review a major
proposal for a new housing project designed to pro-
vide housing for employees.

CONCLUSION

I hope these new facts and my questions will help you provide
me with the clear direction I desire. In preparing this
reply I began to be troubled by the thought that almost any
restriction your opinion may impose begins to relate to the
philosophy of my employer or to my own philosophy as to why
to go to work for COLAB in the first place.

Very truly yours,

Linda Best
146 Via Copla
Alamo, CA 94507
(415) 837-7775

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 25814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

June 26, 1981

A-81-032

Linda Best
146 Via Copla
Alamo, CA  94507

Dear Linda:

Thank you for your letter of June 3, 1981, requesting
advice from this office as to your duties under the Political
Reform Act. This advice is provided pursuant to Government
Code Section 83114(b).1/

The facts, as I understand them from your letter and
our discussions, are as follows. You are a member of the
San Ramon Valley Area Planning Commission by appointment of
the Contra Costa County Board of Supervisors. On May 26,
1981, you were hired by COLAB (Coalition of Labor and Business,
Alameda and Contra Costa Counties) to be their Executive
Secretary. COLAB is a non-profit corporation organized by
labor and business interests to promote members' views on
economic and social issues. COLAB is run by a 25-person
Board of Directors elected by the membership. All member
groups are represented on the Board of Directors. COLAB's
membership varies from 75 to_125 members, which includes
both individuals and groups.2/

You stated in your letter that COLAB concentrates on
broad social and economic issues and tends to avoid taking
positions on individual development projects. Examples

_____________________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

2/ Members include the Buildings and Trade Council of
Alameda County; Contra Costa Central Labor Council; Operating
Engineers, Local No. 3; Underground Contractors Association
of the East Bay; Associated Building Industry; Aggregates &
Concrete Association of the East Bay; Contra Costa County
Development Association; East Bay Construction Equipment
Dealers; Plumbers & Gas Fitters, Local No. 444; Plumbing,
Heating, Piping Employers Council; Seamfitters, Local No. 342;
individual builders, developers and contractors, etc.

Linda Best
June 26, 1981
Page Two


given of issues on which COLAB has expended significant
effort since its inception in 1976 are:

     (a) Developing a national air quality plan for the
San Francisco Bay area;

     (b) Responding to several proposals concerning the
exclusive reliance on infilling (as opposed to urban
expansion) as the method for meeting the Bay Area's
housing needs;

     (c) Increasing awareness of the relationship between
jobs and provision of adequate housing;

     (d) Efforts to improve the process by which governmental
entities review proposals for development;

     (e) Efforts to encourage the development of accurate
population and housing needs projections for the San Francisco
Bay area;

     (f) Efforts to insure adequate urban infrastructure to
service the transportation, sewer and water needs of
the Bay area;

     (g) Encouragement of all forms of economic growth and
discouragement of laws, policies or practices which
tend to discourage economic growth for the benefit of
business and labor.

     You also noted that COLAB's activities are confined to
promoting its philosophy on state and regional levels of
government (e.g. with the Association of Bay Area Governments),
and that it never takes positions relative to proposals
before the Planning Commission. However, individual members
or directors of COLAB may appear before the Planning Commission
on specific projects in which they have an interest.

     COLAB is primarily funded by membership dues which
range from $10 to $2,400 or more per year. Our understanding
is that your salary represents 24% of the 1980-1981 budget.

     The questions you posed in your letter concern the
extent to which your employment with COLAB presents the
potential for conflicts of interest.

Linda Best
June 26, 1981
Page Three


     Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act provides
that:

          No public official at any level of state or local
     government shall make, participate in making or in any
     way attempt to use his official position to influence a
     governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
     to know he has a financial interest.

     The Act further provides that an official has a financial
interest for the purposes of disqualification,

          ... if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
     will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
     from its effect on the public generally, on:

                         * * *

          (c)  Any source of income, ... aggregating two
     hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided,
     to, received by or promised to the public official
     within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
     made.

                    Section 87103.

     Because COLAB as an organization pays you more than
$250 in salary per year, it is a "source of income" to you
within the meaning of the Act.  In 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702 (copy enclosed), the Commission has established guidelines
for determining when a decision will have a material financial
effect upon an official's financial interest.  2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18702(b)(3)(B) provides that in the case of a
source of income of $250 or more, an official must disqualify
herself if "there is a nexus between the governmental decision
and the purpose for which the official receives income...."
In other words, the regulation prevents you from accomplishing
as an official what you have been paid to do as an advocate.

     Since part of your duties as Executive Secretary is to
lobby for COLAB's positions on governmental action, and
actions you would take on the Planning Commission in connection
with those positions could foreseeably further the policies
of COLAB; there would therefore be a nexus between your
actions on the Planning Commission and your duties as Executive

Linda Best
June 26, 1981
Page Four


Secretary of COLAB. For example, if COLAB took a position
on the issue of slope density requirements, you would have
to refrain from participating in a Planning Commission decision
on the slope density ordinance.2/ Or, if COLAB worked for
changes in the process by which governmental entities reviewed
proposals for development, you could not participate in
Planning Commission decisions involving the process by which
the Commission reviewed proposals for development. And,
clearly, if COLAB did take a position relative to a specific
development or other type of proposal before the Planning
Commission for review, you would be required to disqualify
yourself from participating in the review of that proposal.

You also asked whether the Political Reform Act prohibits
your participating in Planning Commission decisions involving
a member and/or director of COLAB in his or her personal
capacity. The resolution to this question depends on whether
the member and/or director is a source of income to you
within the meaning of the Act. After discussion, the staff
has concluded that, in this instance at least, the Act does
not contemplate piercing a non-profit corporation such as
COLAB to treat a member or director as a source of income to
an employee of that non-profit association such as yourself.
Thus you would not have to disqualify yourself from partici­
pating in decisions affecting a member or director of COLAB
solely on the basis of their membership or directorship of
COLAB.

I trust that this discussion has been helpful. It
should be noted that the Fair Political Practices Commission
does not have jurisdiction over other laws pertaining to
conflicts of interest. If there are other laws that would
require that you disqualify yourself from participating in a
Planning Commission decision involving members or directors

_____________________________

     3/ Participation in a governmental decision includes
not only voting, but also participating in deliberations,
conferring with other members of the governing body, and
chairing meetings of the body. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18700 and Opinion requested by Vincent F. Biondo, 1 FPPC
Opinions 54 (No. 75-036, July 2, 1975) (copies enclosed).

Linda Best
June 26, 1981
Page Five


of COLAB, the Political Reform Act would not prohibit those
laws from taking effect. If I can be of further assistance,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosures

FPPC
JUN 5 8 44 AM '81

June 3, 1981


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC)
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Attention:  Ms. Diane Fishburn

Dear Ms. Fishburn:

The purpose of this letter is to formally request an advisory
opinion from the FPPC as discussed by phone last week.

I serve as a volunteer member of the San Ramon Valley Area
Planning Commission (SRVAPC), by appointment of the Contra
Costa County Board of Supervisors. The commission was
established by the Board of Supervisors in 1977 under the
authority of Government Code Section 65100(c). I have served
on the commission since its inception and am presently the
commission's chairperson.

The duties of the SRVAPC are as provided in Government Code
Section 65101. These duties include recommendations to the
Board of Supervisors concerning rezoning, general plan
changes and other ordinances involving land use in the San
Ramon Valley area. The SRVAPC also has original jurisdiction
concerning use permits, development plans and major sub-
division maps within the same geographic area.

On May 26, 1981 I was employed as the "executive secretary"
for COLAB, (The Coalition of Labor and Business, Alameda and
Contra Costa Counties). I am in training to replace the person
who has held the position since 1977. COLAB's offices
are located at 1030 Shary Court, Suite B, Concord, CA 94518.
In accepting the position with COLAB I assumed that I could
remain on the SRVAPC. The SRVAPC meets on two nights per
month and my duties at COLAB are 8 a.m. to 4 p.m.

In accepting the COLAB position I believed that there would
be no "legal" conflict of interest arising from my service on
the SRVAPC because:

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
June 2, 1981
Page -2-


     (a)  COLAB is a broad based non-profit corporation pri-
marily interested in matters of general application; and

     (b)  because there could be no direct financial gain or
loss to me arising from my service on the S.R.V.A.P.C.
or any vote arising there;

My preliminary assessment has been sharply questioned in a
recent local newspaper article and I have decided to seek an
advisory opinion from the F.P.P.C. in order to remove all
doubt.

There have been no meetings of the SRVAPC since I started
with COLAB, and pending your opinion I have decided to
refrain from participating in any SRVAPC deliberations.
Accordingly, I would like your reply as soon as possible.  To
assist you in your evaluation I enclose a statement from
Bonnie England, current Executive Secretary of COLAB.

     Specifically, I would like your answer to the following
questions, first concerning those matters upon which the
S.R.V.P.C. has original jurisdiction (tentative maps, etc.)
and secondly, upon those matters upon which the SRVAPC is
advisory only to the Board of Supervisors (zone changes,
general plan changes and other ordinances).  May I legally
vote and participate as a member of the SRVAPC concerning a
matter or proposal involving:

     (1)  lands owned by an individual who is a member of a
group, organization or union which is an organizational
member of COLAB;

     (2)  A proposal or project upon which the COLAB
Board of Directors has taken an official position;

     (3)  Lands where the landowner or developer is a dues
paying member of COLAB;

     (4)  Lands where the landowner or developer is an
elected member of COLAB's 25 person Board of Directors;

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
June 2, 1981
Page -3-


(5) Lands where a COLAB board member or a general
member of COLAB is an independent contractor repre-
senting the landowner (such as an attorney, architect or
engineer);

(6) Lands where a board member or member of COLAB is a
supplier, contractor or material man who may stand to
gain if the matter is approved;

(7) Lands which are proximate to a COLAB member's
land or a COLAB member's business;

(8) Lands owned by a COLAB supplier, consultant or
other contractors.

I assume my set of questions are complete but you should
expand your opinion to include all situations so I am free to
serve with clear direction.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this request. I may
be contacted at either my home (415)837-7775 or at the
C.O.L.A.B. Offices (415)676-3272.

Very truly yours,

Linda Best
146 Via Copla
Alamo, CA 94507

.  ., I

COLAB
COALITION OF LABOR AND BUSINESS
ALAMEDA & CONTRA COSTA COUNTIES
FOUNDER MEMBERS
1030 Shary Court, Suite B - Concord, California - 94520
Associated Building Industry
415/676-3272
Aggregates & Concrete Assoc
East Bay
Alameda County
Building Trades Council
Associated General Contractors
East Bay
June 3, 1981
Ironworkers
Building Trades Council
Contra Costa
Central Labor Council
Contra Costa County
Development Association
The Contractors
Equipment Dealers
Teamsters Augmented Local No 3
Plumbers & Gas Fitters
Local No 444

TO:      Whom It May Concern

Plumbing Heating Piping
Employees Council
FROM:    Bonnie England, COLAB Executive Secretary
Steamfitters Local No 342
Underground Contractors
Association East Bay

SUBJECT:  COLAB's Organization/Activities/Finances


PREFACE

     Linda Best, COLAB's new executive secretary, has requested
that the within information be provided to assist her in
clarifying a question raised by the press concerning her con-
tinued service on the San Ramon Valley Area Planning
Commission.  Linda started with COLAB on May 26, 1981.

     COLAB has never had the occasion to appear at the SRVAPC and
believes that what Linda does with her own free time is a
matter of personal choice.  On the other hand, we would have
grave concern if any employee of COLAB or any member organi-
zation was precluded from public service or from voting on a
matter involving a single member.

     Added information will be provided upon request directed to
COLAB's office.


ORGANIZATIONAL BACKGROUND

     COLAB is a non-profit corporation founded in 1976.  COLAB is
a two county-private-non-partisan-educational coalition of
labor and business interests devoted to the belief that
environmental regulations have tended to ignore other economic
and social objectives.  The organization (which has
existed for five years) and COLAB's member organizations
represent several thousand persons, families and businesses
within Alameda and Contra Costa County.


                                                            -1-

COLAB is operated by a 25 person Board of Directors elected
by the membership. All member groups are represented on the
Board of Directors. COLAB's membership varies from time to
time from between 75 to 125 members.

The general activity of COLAB consists of the following:

(a) monthly meetings whereby the member organizations
coordinate activities and exchange ideas, information and strategy; and

(b) research, reports, papers and speakers wherein the
members are kept advised of the economic and social
effects of national, state, regional and local projects or proposals which tend to restrict economic
growth;

In its activities, COLAB has concentrated on broad issues and
has tended to avoid taking positions on individual development projects. The reasons for this are threefold. First,
COLAB lacks the resources to get directly involved in the
details of individual development projects. Second, COLAB
generally believes that COLAB is more effective if it avoids
becoming embroiled in local issues. Third, COLAB members
understand that COLAB is intended to get involved only in
issues of general significance.

Issues on which COLAB has expended significant effort since
its inception include:

(a) Developing a national air quality plan for the San
Francisco Bay area;

(b) Responding to several proposals concerning the
exclusive reliance on infilling (as opposed to urban
expansion) as the method for meeting the Bay Area's
housing needs;

(c) Increasing awareness of the relationship between
jobs and the provision of adequate housing;

(d) Efforts to improve the process by which governmental entities review proposals for development;

(e) Efforts to encourage the development of accurate
population and housing needs projections for the San
Francisco Bay area;

(f) Efforts to insure adequate urban infrastructure to
service the transportation, sewer and water needs of the
Bay Area;

-2-

(g) Encouragement of all forms of economic growth and
discouragement of laws, policies or practices which tend
to discourage economic growth for the benefit of busi-
ness and labor.

COLAB's Board of Directors, all active in their own right,
help to formulate and carry out COLAB's policies. COLAB's
overall budget and staffing are allocated as follows: 60%
organizational; 20% communications; and 20% research. Linda
Best's (COLAB's new executive secretary) salary is 24% of
COLAB's projected 1980-81 budget.

MEMBERSHIP PROFILE

The following information (based upon 1980 sources of income)
provides a profile of COLAB's paid membership. Care must be
taken to recognize that many of COLAB's member organizations
have special status independent of paying dues.

1980 Paid Membership Breakdown by Categories

                               members          % of paid membership income

General Business &
Industry                        15.7% ......................    15%

Contractors                     15.7% ......................    20%

Builders/Developers             14.4% ......................    10%

Labor Unions                    13.1% ......................     3%

Associations                     9.2% ......................    13%

Individuals                      9.2% ......................    .5%

Buildings Materials             7.8% ......................    32%

Engineers                        5.2% ......................     2%

Equipment Suppliers             5.2% ......................     3%

Realtors                         1.0% ......................    .5%
                               100.0%                       100.0%


-3-

sections.question:
s, first concerning those matters upon which the 
S.R.V.P.C. has original jurisdiction (tentative maps, etc.) 
and secondly, upon those matters upon which the SRVAPC is 
advisory only to the Board of Supervisors (zone changes, 
general plan changes and other ordinances). 
May I legally 
vote and participate as a member of the SRVAPC concerning a 
matter or proposal involving: 
(l) 
lands owned by an individual who is a member of a 
group, organization or union which is an organizational 
member of COLAB; 
(2) 
A proposal or project upon which the COLAB 
Board of Directors has taken an official position; 
(3) 
Lands where the landowner or developer is a dues 
paying member of COLAB; 
(4) 
Lands where the landowner or developer is an 
elected member of COLAS's 25 person Board of Directors; 


State of California 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
June 2, 1981 
Page -3-
(5) 
Lands where a COLAB board member or a general 
member of COLAB is an independent contractor repre-
senting the landowner (such as an attorney, architect or 
engineer) ; 
(6) 
Lands where a board member or member of COLAB is a 
supplier, contractor or material man who may stand to 
gain if the matter is approved; 
(7) 
Lands which are proximate to a COLAB member's 
land or a COLAB member's business; 
(8) 
Lands owned by a COLAB supplier, consultant or 
other contractors. 
I assume my set of questions are complete but you should 
expand your opinion to include all situations so I am free to 
serve with clear direction. 
Thank you for your prompt attention to this request. 
I may 
be contacted at either my home (415)837-7775 or at the 
C.O.L.A.B. Offices (415)676-3272.
sections.conclusion:
I hope these new facts and my questions will help you provide 
me with the clear direction I desire. 
In preparing this 
reply I began to be troubled by the thought that almost any 
restriction your opinion may impose begins to relate to the 
philosophy of my employer or to my own philosophy in agreeing 
to go to work for COLAB in the first place.

QUESTION: s, first concerning those matters upon which the 
S.R.V.P.C. has original jurisdiction (tentative maps, etc.) 
and secondly, upon those matters upon which the SRVAPC is 
advisory only to the Board of Supervisors (zone changes, 
general plan changes and other ordinances). 
May I legally 
vote and participate as a member of the SRVAPC concerning a 
matter or proposal involving: 
(l) 
lands owned by an individual who is a member of a 
group, organization or union which is an organizational 
member of COLAB; 
(2) 
A proposal or project upon which the COLAB 
Board of Directors has taken an official position; 
(3) 
Lands where the landowner or developer is a dues 
paying member of COLAB; 
(4) 
Lands where the landowner or developer is an 
elected member of COLAS's 25 person Board of Directors; 


State of California 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
June 2, 1981 
Page -3-
(5) 
Lands where a COLAB board member or a general 
member of COLAB is an independent contractor repre-
senting the landowner (such as an attorney, architect or 
engineer) ; 
(6) 
Lands where a board member or member of COLAB is a 
supplier, contractor or material man who may stand to 
gain if the matter is approved; 
(7) 
Lands which are proximate to a COLAB member's 
land or a COLAB member's business; 
(8) 
Lands owned by a COLAB supplier, consultant or 
other contractors. 
I assume my set of questions are complete but you should 
expand your opinion to include all situations so I am free to 
serve with clear direction. 
Thank you for your prompt attention to this request. 
I may 
be contacted at either my home (415)837-7775 or at the 
C.O.L.A.B. Offices (415)676-3272.

CONCLUSION: I hope these new facts and my questions will help you provide 
me with the clear direction I desire. 
In preparing this 
reply I began to be troubled by the thought that almost any 
restriction your opinion may impose begins to relate to the 
philosophy of my employer or to my own philosophy in agreeing 
to go to work for COLAB in the first place.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-4441 322-6444 August 21, 1981 4-81-032 Linda Best 146 Via Copla Alamo, CA 94507 Dear Linda: This letter is sent in response to your letter of July 17, 1981, in which you requested clarification and ampliation of my advice letter to you dated June 26, 1981. Generally, our policy is not to advise regarding future hypothetical situations since the analysis of conflicts of interest depends heavily on the concrete factual situation presented. However, I will attempt to answer your questions. If in the future you need advice as to a particular decision, we will be most happy to provide you with it. As you noted, my understanding of the situation as reflected in my letter was that your duties included assisting in the presentation of and presenting COLAB's position to governmental officials and decision-making bodies in an attempt to influence governmental decisions. However, if your duties are purely administrative, my analysis of the application of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(B) to your situation should be disregarded. On the other hand, if your position with COLAB does require your participation in the research, development, implementation or preparation of policy or position statements or the promotion or discussion of COLAB's policies or positions with public officials (whether or not you actually formulate the policies), my analysis stands. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(B) provides that an official has a financial interest in a governmental decision when "there is a nexus between the governmental decision and the purpose for which the official received income." As you noted, the word "nexus" means a tie, link, or connection; accordingly, if a person is paid to promote or advocate the policies or position of an individual or group, ne or she Linda Best August 21, 1981 Page Two may not then participate in a governmental decision which draws into consideration that policy or position. I have enclosed a copy of a recent advice letter in which the "nexus" test was applied. The underlying rationale for the "nexus" test is that when a connection exists between a person's job and his or her role as a public official, there is a presumption that the value of the employee's services to the employer is based at least in part on the fact that the employee is a public official. If an employee is being paid to do something similar to what she does as a public official, we presume that the employer is getting something of value from the situation. There is an inherent appearance of conflict as well as an actual conflict when an official is being paid to promote or represent certain positions, on the one hand, and on the other, she is being called on to determine public policies and positions
